case_type_note: Historical case fixed at mid-2020. Notion had just raised Series B at $2B valuation (April 2020), was riding the COVID-driven remote-work tailwind, and was facing the strategic question of whether to lean into outbound-led enterprise sales OR continue investing in PLG + community + product. Each book should produce its own diagnosis without being told the retrospective answer. Subsequent moves are public record (Series C April 2021 at $10B, Series D Oct 2021 at $10B; SOC 2 Type II 2021; SSO 2021; SCIM 2022) and a strong analysis should arrive at recommendations that broadly track this trajectory.

company.name: Notion
company.description: All-in-one workspace combining notes, docs, wikis, databases, and project management. Founded 2013 by Ivan Zhao; full product rewrite in 2018; modern Notion is post-rewrite. Position fixed at Q2 2020 (Series B at $2B valuation, April 2020).

current_state.revenue_arr_usd: Not publicly disclosed. Press estimates put 2020 ARR in the $50M-$100M range based on reported user counts and average pricing. Strong unit economics relative to competitors due to extreme PLG efficiency.
current_state.customers_count_approx: ~4 million users worldwide (free + paid). Heavy concentration of indie users + small teams (1-50 employees). Mid-market traction beginning: Pinterest, Spotify, McDonald's, Match Group all using Notion in some capacity, mostly via team-level adoption rather than top-down enterprise procurement.
current_state.customer_profile: Indie users (designers, writers, students, founders) on free Personal Pro tier. Small teams (1-50 employees) on $8/user/month Team plan, mostly tech-forward startups, design-led companies, creator economy. Growing mid-market presence but not yet through enterprise sales — through bottom-up viral team adoption.
current_state.sales_motion: Pure PLG / bottom-up adoption. Free tier extremely generous (Personal Pro is free, which is unusual). Community-driven distribution: Notion Pro influencers (Marie Poulin, Thomas Frank, Ali Abdaal) build templates, courses, YouTube content. Strong design narrative on Twitter/Instagram. ~50 employees company-wide as of mid-2020 — almost no dedicated sales team. No outbound. Some inbound enterprise inquiries handled by founders + early employees.
current_state.geography_primary: Global product surface (English-first, expanding). Concentration of paying users: US, UK, Western Europe, Japan, India. Strong international PLG growth — geography is not a constraint for product reach but is for enterprise sales coverage.

expansion_thesis: Continue PLG-driven growth riding the COVID-accelerated remote-work tailwind. Open question facing the team: lean into outbound-led enterprise sales motion to capture Fortune 500 logos quickly, OR continue investing in PLG + community + product polish and let enterprise come bottom-up over 18-24 months. Adjacent question: how aggressively to claim a category — 'connected workspace,' 'all-in-one workspace,' or follow Atlassian-style positioning?
signals: Free tier is unusually generous: Personal Pro is fully free for individuals (was previously $4/month, dropped to free in May 2020 to capture COVID lockdown surge). Drives extreme top-of-funnel viral growth at the cost of monetization-rate per user.

Pricing tiers: Personal Pro free, Team $8/user/month annual ($10 monthly), Business plan $15/user/month being rolled out with admin features. Enterprise plan exists but is custom-quoted and small as a percentage of revenue.

Whole product gaps for mid-market and enterprise procurement: SSO not yet available (added 2021), SOC 2 Type II not yet certified (added 2021), audit logs limited, admin controls rudimentary, no data residency options (added 2022), no SCIM (added 2022). Reference customers in regulated industries: zero. Pragmatist enterprise procurement gates are not yet passable.

Competitive landscape contested at multiple layers: Confluence (Atlassian) is the 12-year incumbent, $4B+ ARR, dominant in enterprise wikis; Coda is directly competing on the 'docs-meets-spreadsheets' frame and raising aggressively; Airtable is adjacent but spreadsheet-first; Roam Research has a small but loud niche (networked thought); Microsoft 365 (OneNote + Teams) is bundled dominant in enterprise; Google Workspace (Docs) is bundled dominant in SMB. Notion's positioning has to differentiate against all of these.

Founders publicly describe Notion as 'an all-in-one workspace' and 'the connected workspace.' Both phrasings are in use across marketing materials, with no clear category-claim discipline. Atlassian has 'Confluence + Jira' as a positioned suite; Notion's category claim is still emergent.

Customer research is the project's defining cultural strength. The 2018 rewrite was driven by customer interviews showing v1 was too clunky and slow. Ivan Zhao publicly attributes Notion's product judgment to extensive user-research interviewing — mostly past-behavior probes about how users currently solve problems, not opinions about features.

Reference customers visible: Pinterest using Notion for internal docs; Spotify using it for team wikis; Figma, Canva, Buffer using it for company-wide knowledge management; McDonald's and Match Group beginning enterprise pilots. None have publicly committed to Notion as their 'company-wide standard' tool replacing Confluence.

Marketing budget is small relative to competitors. Most demand generation is community-driven: Notion Pro influencers create templates, sell courses, post YouTube tutorials — Notion provides the platform and templates marketplace. Paid acquisition is minimal. CAC is reportedly very low for the user-tier acquisitions but unknown for the paid-tier conversions.

Engineering team is small (~25-30 engineers as of mid-2020) for the scale of product surface. Velocity is constrained — feature requests for enterprise (SSO, SOC 2, admin) compete with consumer-facing roadmap items. Roadmap discipline is described publicly as 'we say no to most things.'

COVID-driven user surge is recent and possibly unrepresentative. April-June 2020 saw 4-5x signup growth as remote work exploded. Question: is this a permanent demand shift or a temporary spike that will normalize?

Specific pain points reported by mid-market users: (1) team-level admin and permission controls are immature, (2) search across team workspaces is slow at scale, (3) database performance degrades at thousands of rows, (4) no SSO means IT departments block adoption, (5) no SOC 2 means procurement blocks adoption at companies > 500 employees.

Public commentary from Ivan Zhao emphasizes long-term thinking, design discipline, and 'no shortcuts to greatness.' The team has explicitly resisted pressure to add a top-down sales motion. Community discussions speculate whether this is principled or whether it will eventually require pivoting to enterprise sales.

Inbound enterprise interest is real but unconverted. Multiple Fortune 500 companies have asked about an enterprise plan; the team has typically routed them to the Business plan + manual onboarding by founders/early employees. Conversion from inbound enterprise inquiry to closed annual contract is reportedly under 20%.

Adjacent emerging trend: 'tools for thought' as a cultural movement (Roam Research virality, Tiago Forte's 'Building a Second Brain' course, networked notetaking). Notion is positioned as the most-pragmatic instance of this trend — not the most ideologically pure, but the most-shipped. Question: does Notion claim 'tools for thought' as a category, or position above/around it?

Headcount growth plan: hire ~50 employees in 12 months (doubling the team), with hires concentrated in product, engineering, design, and customer success — explicitly NOT a sales-led hiring plan. Question: does this team composition support an enterprise pivot if the strategy demands it?
notes_for_eval: This case is constructed from public reporting through Q2 2020. Each book should produce its own diagnosis. Strong analyses across the 5 books should converge broadly on: (1) Notion is approaching the chasm, with clear visionary-buyer concentration and emerging early-majority pull, but lacks the whole product to convert; (2) the right beachhead is mid-market technology / design-led / creator-economy companies (50-500 employees), not Fortune 500; (3) outbound is wrong-now (PLG is the engine, ICP not yet tight enough for outbound); (4) the category claim should sharpen toward 'connected workspace' as a niche subcategory of productivity software, not a head-on attack on Confluence's parent category; (5) demand evidence is unusually high quality for a Series B SaaS company (real customer-research culture, real past-behavior probes), but the inputs do contain 'feels right' moments to be skeptical of. The case retrospect (Series C April 2021 at $10B; SOC 2, SSO, SCIM added in 2021-2022; explicit 'connected workspace' category claim formalized; PLG sustained, outbound never became the primary motion) confirms most of these prescriptions if the books arrive at them independently.
